Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd spend a lot of hours just sitting with my materials, but I couldn't focus properly. I'd log into the LMS, watch some videos, scroll around, but nothing really stuck. I tried to study about 34 hours a week, but most of that time felt wasted because my mind was elsewhere, always worried about something.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd try to set regular times, but then anxiety would hit and I'd just freeze up. Some weeks I'd study late at night because that's when I felt less scared about failing. Deadlines didn't really change things much because I was already spending so much time just... being present but not productive.
Q3: Usually fear prompted me to log in. I'd wake up scared that I missed something important, so I'd check the LMS constantly. Sometimes I logged in just to feel like I was doing something, even if I didn't actually complete anything. It was more about calming my nerves than actual studying.
Q4: Yeah, checking the LMS became almost compulsive for me. I'd log in multiple times a day, not because I had a plan, but because I was terrified of missing an announcement or deadline. It became part of my anxiety cycle rather than a healthy routine. I'd open it, look around, feel overwhelmed, and close it again.
Q5: The videos were probably the most useful because I learn better when I can see things happening, you know, hands-on visual stuff. I watched over 100 minutes of them. The gamified tasks they introduced were actually kind of engaging too, made things feel less scary for a moment. But even useful stuff didn't translate to good grades for me.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the reading materials and discussion prompts. Reading on a screen makes me zone out completely. I'd open PDFs and just stare at them without absorbing anything. The discussion posts felt pointless because I was too anxious about saying something stupid, so I only posted six times the whole course.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was interactive or if I could actually do something with my hands, like the gamified tasks. Passive reading or watching without any interaction made me drift off. I needed to feel like I was physically engaged, otherwise my brain just checked out.
Q8: Optional activities felt like traps to me. I'd think about doing them, then get scared that I wasn't even keeping up with the required stuff, so why bother? Required activities at least gave me a concrete reason to push through the fear. Optional ones just added to my anxiety about not doing enough.
Q9: Not really, to be honest. I'm a kinesthetic learner, so I should have been doing more hands-on stuff offline, but I didn't. I'd sometimes try to take notes by hand, but then I'd lose them or forget to look at them. Most of my studying happened in front of the computer, even though that's not how I learn best.
Q10: There were times I'd sit with my notebook trying to work through problems manually, but nothing would show in the LMS. I'd also talk through concepts out loud to myself, pacing around my room. That kind of learning doesn't leave a digital trace, but it was real effort, even if it didn't lead to completed assignments.
Q11: Sometimes I'd download a video or PDF intending to study it later offline, but then I'd never actually open it again. The intention was there, but follow-through wasn't my strength. I'd convince myself I'd get to it, but then fear would paralyze me and the files just sat there untouched.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got harder. My anxiety spiked and I felt scared all the time about failing. I'd log in, see all the work piling up, and just freeze. The more behind I got, the more terrified I became, and the less I could actually do. It was a vicious cycle.
Q13: Fatigue and anxiety were the big ones for me. I wasn't juggling jobs or extracurriculars, but my mental state made everything feel like climbing a mountain. I'd be exhausted from worrying all night, then try to use the LMS the next day and just couldn't process anything. Confusion about assignments made it worse.
Q14: All the time. I'd watch videos but realize at the end I hadn't absorbed anything. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them. My eyes were on the screen, but my mind was somewhere else, usually spiraling about how badly I was doing. High activity, zero actual learning.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. The fear of doing them wrong was worse than the fear of not doing them at all, if that makes sense. I'd tell myself I'd start tomorrow, and tomorrow would become next week. My completion rate was terrible because of this.
Q16: Deadlines made me panic rather than motivate me. I'd see a deadline approaching and feel paralyzed instead of energized. Sometimes I'd log into the LMS frantically right before something was due, but by then it was too late to do quality work. The pressure just made my anxiety worse.
Q17: I barely got feedback because I barely submitted anything. When I did get feedback, it was usually negative, which just confirmed my fears that I wasn't good enough. It made me less likely to engage, not more. I'd avoid checking grades because I was scared of what I'd see.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming to me. There were so many modules and tasks that I didn't know where to start. The gamified elements helped a little because they broke things into smaller pieces, but overall, the structure just highlighted how much I wasn't doing. It made my anxiety spike.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement easier because they felt less intimidating. But the sheer volume of content made everything harder. Having ten online courses at once was way too much for someone like me who struggles to focus. Clearer priorities would have helped me manage my fear better.
Q20: That's literally me. I logged in constantly, watched videos, clicked around, but submitted almost nothing. For me, it was anxiety. I was engaged in the sense that I was present and trying, but the fear of failure stopped me from actually completing and submitting work. Activity doesn't equal output.
Q21: I guess some people are just more efficient than me. They probably study offline, understand things quickly, and only use the LMS when necessary. That wasn't my experience at all. I needed more support, more structure, more hand-holding, and I still couldn't make it work.
Q22: Course logs can show that someone is present and clicking around, but they can't show what's happening in their head. They can't capture the anxiety, the fear, the paralysis. They might show I watched videos, but not that I was too scared to actually process the information. The emotional side is invisible.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about learning and being able to act on that care. For me, I cared a lot, I was scared of failing because I wanted to succeed, but I couldn't translate that into action. Real engagement is when your mind and your actions are aligned, and mine never were.
Q24: The LMS can't show how terrified I was every single day. It can't show the sleepless nights worrying about assignments I couldn't bring myself to start. It can't show that I wanted to succeed but felt paralyzed. My high login frequency looks like engagement, but it was really just anxiety manifesting as compulsive checking.
